text: The electroreduction of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is catalysed by short-lived, dynamic surface structures that feature sites with anomalously high CO binding energies. Using oxide-derived copper electrodes synthesized via hydrogen reduction of Cu2O precursors, we find that CO can be efficiently reduced to acetate and ethanol with nearly 50% faradaic efficiency at moderate overpotential. Using temperature-programmed desorption (TPD), we elucidate the presence of dynamic Cu-xO surface structures with highly energetic CO binding sites that are significantly greater than those measured on Cu terrace sites and Cu(111)/Cu(100) stepped sites on a polycrystalline copper foil. Following thermal annealing at 350 °C, we observe a 44-fold reduction in surface area normalized current and a decrease in faradaic efficiency below 5%. The loss in performance coincides with the removal of the highly energetic CO binding sites attributed to grain boundary structures that may serve to stabilize the catalytically active sites. Elucidating these structures and the underlying surface chemistry required for CO reduction will aid in the development of efficient catalysts.
